attributes.stem:
A seven-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her parent for review of a persistent cough. Her school had requested that the patient is medically reviewed after her teacher noted the cough constantly for the past 4 weeks. During the consultation, you notice multiple circular burns on her forearms that appear to be cigarette burns. When asked about these marks, the child becomes withdrawn and the parent states they occurred while cooking together. The child appears clean but underweight. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears anxious with vital signs within normal limits. In addition to the burns, there are several bruises of varying ages on her back and thighs.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Document findings and schedule follow-up review
Refer to a paediatrician for assessment
Discuss concerns directly with the parent
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents clear evidence of suspected child physical abuse, including suspicious injuries (cigarette burns and bruises of varying ages) and concerning behaviour (child becoming withdrawn when questioned). As a healthcare professional, there is a legal obligation to report suspected child abuse. In Northern Territory, the legal obligation to report child abuse is on every adult. The specific type of abuse (physical, sexual, emotional, neglect and exposure to family violence) that needs to be legally reported varies between states and territories too, however physical and sexual abuse must always be reported in any location. Furthermore, the age of the child differs between location though is generally up to the age of 16 - 18 years.

Notifying child protection services immediately is the most appropriate action. The presence of suspicious injuries, inconsistent explanation, and the child's behaviour constitute reasonable grounds to suspect significant harm, meeting the threshold for mandatory reporting. The safety of the child is the primary concern. The health practitioner will be protected by confidentiality and liability. If there is immediate urgent risk of harm, then the emergency services (000) should be contacted instead.

Documenting findings and scheduling follow-up review alone is insufficient and potentially dangerous as it delays intervention for a child who may be at immediate risk. While documentation is important, it should not delay reporting.

Referring to a paediatrician may be appropriate as part of ongoing management but is not the immediate action, but it should not delay the mandatory reporting process when there are reasonable grounds to suspect abuse.

Discussing concerns directly with the parent could potentially place the child at greater risk if the parent is the perpetrator. It may also compromise any subsequent investigation by alerting the potential perpetrator.

Arranging for social work review is inadequate as a first step when there are clear indicators of physical abuse requiring immediate statutory intervention. While social work involvement may be valuable, it should not replace the mandatory reporting obligation.

Healthcare professionals have a legal and ethical obligation to report suspected child abuse. The threshold for reporting is reasonable grounds to believe that a child is at risk of significant harm - that is, firm evidence is not needed to report. The report should be made even if uncertain, as the responsibility for investigation lies with child protection services.
learningPoints: Healthcare professionals have a legal obligation to report suspected child abuse or neglect to child protection services.
Physical abuse, sexual abuse, emotional abuse, and neglect are all reportable forms of child maltreatment.
The threshold for mandatory reporting is reasonable grounds to believe that a child is at risk of significant harm.
references: Australian Institute of Family Studies. Mandatory reporting of child abuse and neglect. Australian Government Australian Institute of Family Studies. 2024. Available at: https://aifs.gov.au/resources/resource-sheets/mandatory-reporting-child-abuse-and-neglect

Royal Australian College of General Practioner's. Abuse and violence: working with our patients in general practice, 5th edition (the White Book).  2022.  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/key-racgp-guidelines/view-all-racgp-guidelines/abuse-and-violence/children-and-young-people/child-abuse-and-neglect
